chunks.text: Organophosphate (OP) compounds inhibit acetylcholinesterase, producing a cholinergic crisis with muscarinic and nicotinic effects. Civilian exposures: agricultural pesticides (parathion, malathion, chlorpyrifos, methamidophos, dichlorvos); chemical weapons (sarin, VX, tabun, soman). Clinical SLUDGE-M: Salivation, Lacrimation, Urination, Defecation, GI distress, Emesis, plus Miosis (pinpoint pupils — pathognomonic), bronchospasm, bradycardia, fasciculations, weakness, seizures, respiratory failure. Vapor exposure produces symptoms within minutes; liquid skin contact has 30 min to 18 h delayed onset depending on agent. Field decontamination: remove clothing (90% of dermal contamination); copious water flush with mild soap; RSDL kit (Reactive Skin Decontamination Lotion) for military / advanced civilian responders. Antidotes: ATROPINE 2-6 mg IV in adults, doubled every 5 min until secretions dry (NOT to heart rate or pupil — eyes may stay miotic for hours after systemic recovery); 2-PAM (pralidoxime) 1-2 g IV in adults given as soon as possible to reverse aging of cholinesterase-OP bond; benzodiazepines (diazepam 5-10 mg, midazolam 2-5 mg IV) for seizures or pre-emptively in severe exposure. Atropine is consumed in massive quantities — typical mass-casualty stockpile assumes 10-20 mg per adult patient. Auto-injectors (DuoDote, ATNAA) deliver atropine + 2-PAM in one device for rapid field administration.

Cyanide poisoning occurs from industrial exposure (electroplating, mining, plastics manufacturing producing acrylonitrile), structure fires (combustion of nitrogen-containing materials including wool, silk, nylon, polyurethane foam, melamine — extremely common in modern building fires), and rare chemical attack. Hydrogen cyanide gas (HCN, UN1051) per ERG Guide 117. Mechanism: cyanide binds cytochrome oxidase, halting cellular oxygen utilization; cells suffocate despite normal SaO2 and PaO2. Clinical: bitter almond breath (60% of population genetically unable to detect); rapid CNS effects (anxiety, headache, confusion, seizure, coma); cherry-red skin from venous oxygenation; lactic acidosis on blood gas; cardiovascular collapse. Pulse oximetry FALSELY NORMAL (similar to CO). Smoke inhalation patients with persistent altered mental status, hypotension, and severe lactic acidosis (>10 mmol/L) should be presumed cyanide-toxic and treated empirically. Antidotes: HYDROXOCOBALAMIN (Cyanokit) 5 g IV — preferred in fire scenes because safe with co-existing CO poisoning; turns urine and skin red for 24-48 h. Older Cyanide Antidote Kit (sodium nitrite + sodium thiosulfate) is contraindicated in CO + cyanide co-exposure because nitrite causes methemoglobinemia worsening tissue hypoxia. Field hydroxocobalamin pre-positioned in fire department BLS / ALS kits in increasing jurisdictions.

Mixing chlorine bleach (sodium hypochlorite NaOCl, ~5% in household) with ammonia-containing cleaners (or with urine in domestic incidents) produces chloramine vapors (NH2Cl, NHCl2, NCl3) — a respiratory irritant and known cause of accidental household toxic exposures. Mixing bleach with acidic cleaners (vinegar, hydrochloric acid based toilet bowl cleaners, phosphoric acid descalers) liberates chlorine gas Cl2. Both scenarios produce hospital-presenting toxic exposures, especially during cleaning surges (post-disaster decontamination, COVID-era surface disinfection, food-service deep cleaning). Clinical: immediate eye/nose/throat irritation, cough; pulmonary edema may develop over 6-24 h after high-dose exposure even with mild initial symptoms (delayed phase risk). Field management: remove from exposure; high-flow oxygen; bronchodilators (albuterol nebulizer) for wheeze; observation 6+ hours minimum for delayed pulmonary edema in significant exposure; CXR at 6 h benchmark. Avoid endotracheal intubation if possible because it can worsen airway irritation in awake patient; CPAP with humidified oxygen often adequate. Public-health prevention: clear messaging on cleaner labels, disaster cleanup briefings should explicitly warn against mixing.

Silane (SiH4, UN2203) is a pyrophoric gas — ignites spontaneously on contact with air, often violently. Used widely in semiconductor manufacturing (silicon wafer deposition, thin-film coatings) — Hsinchu, Tainan, Kaohsiung Taiwan; SK Hynix in Korea; TSMC global facilities. Released silane forms a self-igniting fireball with explosive transient overpressure; combustion product is amorphous silicon dioxide (silica) particulate that propagates as smoke. ERG Guide 116 small spill: isolate 100 m all directions; large spill 500 m; large fire engulfment 1600 m and let burn (do not extinguish — flame is the only safe exposure for accumulated silane). PPE: structural turnout with SCBA for outer hot zone; full Level A for source approach (rare — usually let burn). Tactic: STOP flow at the source via remote isolation valve if accessible; cool surrounding equipment with water spray; do NOT direct water at burning silane (steam expansion can disperse cloud and re-ignite at distance). Health: thermal burns from flash; SiO2 particulate inhalation causes pulmonary irritation. Co-located gases (arsine, phosphine, diborane) may release simultaneously in semiconductor fab incident — multi-gas detection essential.

Arsine (AsH3, UN2188) and phosphine (PH3, UN2199) are the most acutely toxic gases used in semiconductor doping and fumigation respectively. ERG Guide 119 (highly toxic + flammable): small spill isolation 100 m, large spill 600 m, downwind protection out to 7 km night for arsine. Both gases are colorless, near-odorless at toxic concentrations (faint garlic / fish smell at high levels — unreliable warning). Arsine mechanism: massive intravascular hemolysis 2-24 h post-exposure, with hemoglobinuria, acute kidney injury, jaundice; lethal exposure can be 10 ppm for 30 min. Phosphine mechanism: cellular respiration inhibition similar to cyanide; lethal at 50 ppm for 60 min; encountered in grain fumigation (aluminum phosphide tablets producing PH3 on hydrolysis), shipping container off-gassing during inspection — multiple Asian port deaths documented. Treatment: supportive — high-flow oxygen, exchange transfusion or hemodialysis for arsine-induced hemolysis, mechanical ventilation as needed for phosphine; no specific antidote for either. Detection: pre-purchased fixed sensors at semiconductor fabs; portable colorimetric tubes (Drager) or electrochemical sensors for incident response.

Industrial radiation sources are the most likely civilian ionizing radiation exposure scenario outside dedicated nuclear incidents. Common encountered sources: cesium-137 in medical radiotherapy and industrial gauges; cobalt-60 in industrial radiography and food irrigation; iridium-192 in non-destructive testing of welds and pipelines (most common worldwide industrial source); strontium-90 in calibration sources; americium-241 in smoke detectors (single source low risk). Lost or stolen sources have caused multiple acute radiation syndrome events: 1987 Goiânia Brazil (Cs-137 from medical machine, 4 dead, 249 contaminated); 2003 Bangkok Thailand (Co-60 from research facility); 2013 Mayapuri India (Co-60 from medical facility scrapped). Operational signature: orphan source typically discovered when responders show unexpected radiation alarms or when first patients present with skin burns, hair loss, GI symptoms, leukopenia 2-72 h post-exposure. Field detection: pancake GM probe screens contamination; NaI scintillator measures gamma exposure rate. Time-Distance-Shielding rule: dose rate inversely proportional to distance squared (doubling distance quarters dose); dense shielding (lead, concrete, steel) reduces gamma. Decon: clothing removal removes 90% surface; full shower; wound flushing before suturing if contamination > 2x background. Treatment of internal contamination: Prussian blue for Cs-137 and Tl, DTPA for transuranic, KI for radioiodine.

Methylene chloride (dichloromethane, UN1593) is a chlorinated solvent used in paint stripping, degreasing, and pharmaceutical manufacturing. ERG Guide 160 small spill: isolate 25 m; large spill 100 m. PPE: nitrile gloves degrade rapidly — butyl rubber required; SCBA for confined-space exposure. Critical hazard: methylene chloride is metabolized in the liver to carbon monoxide, producing significant carboxyhemoglobinemia in addition to direct CNS depression. Symptoms: dizziness, headache, confusion, sometimes delayed by 1-4 h after exposure ends (continuing CO production from absorbed solvent). Pulse oximetry will read normal despite COHb 20-40%. Treatment: high-flow oxygen for hours-to-days (longer than for direct CO inhalation because metabolic CO production continues); consider hyperbaric for COHb >25% or symptomatic; serial COHb measurement at 1, 4, and 12 h. Several fatalities documented in confined-space paint stripping by workers with adequate respiratory protection but no air-supplied SCBA. Industrial deaths in bathtub refinishing operations (small enclosed bathroom + methylene chloride paint stripper) led to EPA bans on consumer products in several jurisdictions.

Anhydrous hydrogen fluoride (HF, UN1052) and aqueous hydrofluoric acid (UN1790) deserve singular attention because dilute solutions (1-5%) can be lethal from small body-surface exposure due to systemic fluoride toxicity. Industrial uses: semiconductor wafer etching (Hsinchu, Taipei, Kaohsiung sites at high frequency), petroleum alkylation refineries, glass etching, rust remover. Pathophysiology: fluoride ion penetrates intact skin and binds calcium/magnesium, producing tissue necrosis disproportionate to visible burn AND systemic hypocalcemia/hypomagnesemia/hyperkalemia with cardiac arrhythmia. Even 2.5% HF over a few percent body surface can be fatal. Clinical signature: severe pain disproportionate to wound appearance, often delayed 1-24 h for dilute solutions. Field treatment: copious water flush 5-15 min ONLY (longer water exposure can spread); apply 2.5% calcium gluconate gel and reapply continuously; for digital burns, subcutaneous calcium gluconate injection 0.5 mL per cm2 burned area; for severe extremity burns, intra-arterial calcium gluconate by physician. Monitor ECG for QT prolongation, T-wave changes; treat hypocalcemia with IV calcium gluconate aggressively (1 g initial, repeat). Industrial first-aid kits in semiconductor and petroleum facilities pre-stock calcium gluconate gel and IV.

Lithium-ion battery thermal events deserve detailed coverage given EV proliferation and energy storage system deployment. Failure modes: mechanical damage (puncture, impact), thermal abuse (external heating, short circuit), electrical abuse (overcharge, deep discharge then recharge), internal defects (manufacturing dendrite formation). Cascade: single cell exotherm initiates adjacent cells, escalating to thermal runaway propagation across pack. Off-gas composition before flaming: hydrogen, carbon monoxide, hydrogen fluoride (HF — major hazard), phosphorus oxyfluoride (POF3), carbonates from electrolyte (DMC, EMC, EC) — all toxic and some explosive. Vapor cloud explosion documented in BESS (battery energy storage system) incidents 2019-2024 worldwide; off-gas pre-flame can sit at sub-LEL until ignition source triggers vapor cloud detonation. Tactical guidance: extreme caution on confined-space EV or BESS fire — vent before approach; HF detection essential (ppm-level cardiac/cardiopulmonary risk); monitor for re-ignition 24-72 h post-extinguishment; isolate from structures 15+ m post-fire. Water requirement to fully cool: 90,000+ L for EV traction battery, much more for BESS. Foam (AFFF) limited utility — water cooling is the operative tactic. Quarantine submerged EVs from saltwater immersion (intrusion + dendrite + thermal runaway risk over days).

Chlorine dioxide (ClO2, UN9191) is encountered in water treatment, paper bleaching, and food disinfection. Critical operational note: chlorine dioxide is sometimes misrepresented in alternative-medicine fraud as 'Miracle Mineral Solution' (MMS) for treating diseases, leading to civilian poisoning incidents requiring MCI-style response in clusters. Industrial release: ERG Guide 143 small spill 50 m, large spill 300 m; oxidizer plus toxic. PPE: Level A for source approach, Level B downwind. Health: respiratory irritation, pulmonary edema, GI bleeding from oral ingestion; methemoglobinemia possible. Treatment: remove from exposure, supportive care, methylene blue 1-2 mg/kg IV for symptomatic methemoglobinemia, fluid resuscitation for hemodynamic instability. Reactivity: violent reaction with organic materials; explosive in concentrated form; light-sensitive degrades to chlorine. Reactivity in storage: cylinders kept upright, ventilated, away from organics; release events often follow valve mechanical failure. EmergencyNet command-side advisor should flag clusters of GI + respiratory symptoms in non-industrial setting as possible MMS / ClO2 ingestion event in addition to traditional foodborne or chemical-attack differential.

Sulfur dioxide (SO2, UN1079) is a colorless gas with sharp pungent odor used in food preservation, refrigeration (older systems), pulp bleaching, and as combustion product of sulfur-containing fossil fuels. Volcanic emission also produces SO2 — relevant in Indonesia, Philippines, Japan operational contexts (Mount Sinabung, Taal, Sakurajima eruptions). ERG Guide 125: small spill isolation 30 m, large spill 200 m, downwind protection 200-1500 m depending on size and conditions. PPE: SCBA mandatory, Level B suit acceptable for splash protection (gas not skin-toxic in non-liquid form). Health: 1-5 ppm causes nasal/throat irritation; 10 ppm cough and chest tightness; 100 ppm immediate severe respiratory irritation; 400+ ppm rapidly fatal. Asthmatics highly sensitive — exacerbation at concentrations tolerated by general population. Reactive Airways Dysfunction Syndrome (RADS) develops in some survivors with persistent reactive airway disease. Treatment: remove to fresh air, high-flow humidified oxygen, bronchodilators (nebulized albuterol), corticosteroids for severe bronchospasm. Liquid SO2 contact causes frostbite. Reacts with water to form sulfurous acid; in atmosphere converts to sulfur trioxide and sulfuric acid mist (acid rain).

Formaldehyde (HCHO, UN1198 solution / UN2209 anhydrous) is encountered in healthcare (pathology, embalming, sterilization), manufacturing (resins, particleboard, plywood, textile finishing), and as combustion product of plastics in building fires. ERG Guide 132 small spill 50 m, large spill 300 m. PPE: Level B for splash, Level A for source. Health: 0.5-1 ppm threshold for irritation; 1-5 ppm watery eyes, throat burning; 10-20 ppm severe lacrimation, cough; 100+ ppm severe pulmonary edema, can be lethal. Carcinogen — IARC Group 1 (nasopharyngeal, possibly leukemia). Skin: contact dermatitis common in healthcare workers and embalmers; sensitization develops over time. Major incident scenarios: pathology lab fume hood failure with multiple workers; embalming chemical spill in funeral home; particleboard factory release (Asian manufacturing concentration); off-gassing from FEMA trailers post-Katrina (well-documented occupational health failure leading to FEMA trailer policy reform). Treatment: remove from exposure, copious water flush of skin/eye, supportive respiratory care, corticosteroids for chemical pneumonitis, no specific antidote. Long-term surveillance for nasopharyngeal carcinoma in chronic exposure populations.

CDC Category A biological agents — highest concern for bioterrorism and high mortality — relevant to mass casualty preparedness even when unlikely. Anthrax (Bacillus anthracis): inhalational form has 80-90% mortality untreated, 45% with aggressive ICU care; biphasic clinical course with prodrome (1-6 days) followed by fulminant phase with mediastinal widening on CXR. Treatment: ciprofloxacin or doxycycline + raxibacumab/obiltoxaximab (anti-toxin); post-exposure prophylaxis 60 days antibiotic + vaccine. Plague (Yersinia pestis) — pneumonic form rapidly fatal; treat with streptomycin or gentamicin or doxycycline; respiratory droplet isolation. Smallpox (Variola major) — eradicated wild-type but stockpile concern; vaccinia vaccine within 4 days post-exposure prevents disease; airborne and contact isolation. Tularemia (Francisella tularensis) — multiple syndromes; streptomycin or gentamicin treatment. Botulism (Clostridium botulinum toxin) — descending flaccid paralysis, no fever; antitoxin reduces progression but cannot reverse paralysis; respiratory support critical. Viral hemorrhagic fevers (Ebola, Marburg, Lassa, others) — supportive care, ribavirin for some arenaviruses; strict barrier precautions including PAPR, double gloves, dedicated equipment. Field and hospital-level isolation policies must be pre-established because improvising during incident loses critical hours.

Synthetic opioids — fentanyl, carfentanil, sufentanil — are encountered in mass casualty contexts following both criminal use (powder release in transit hubs) and overdose surge events (drug supply contamination causing simultaneous overdoses across a region). Carfentanil is ~10,000x morphine potency; veterinary tranquilizer for elephants. 2002 Moscow theater hostage crisis (Russian forces deployed aerosolized carfentanil + remifentanil mixture, ~130 hostage deaths from respiratory depression — case-defining event for opioid mass exposure). Clinical: classic opioid triad (respiratory depression / pinpoint pupils / decreased mental status); rapid onset; victims appear 'turned off'. Field management: secure airway, bag-valve-mask ventilation; NALOXONE 0.4-2 mg IV/IM/IN initial — high doses needed (10-20 mg+) for fentanyl analogs; intravenous infusion may be required because half-life of opioid exceeds naloxone (recurrent depression after initial reversal). Responders: nitrile gloves and N95 minimum for clear powder exposure; don't presume unknown white powder is opioid — could be many things — but always plan opioid as one possibility. Naloxone auto-injector (Evzio) and intranasal Narcan deployed widely in EMS and increasingly in police and fire kits in Asia-Pacific.

Responder radiation dose limits per ICRP and US/Asian regulatory equivalents must be tracked during any radiation incident. Routine occupational limit: 50 mSv per year (5 rem). Emergency response tiers per IAEA / NCRP: 50 mSv (5 rem) for any responder activity in ordinary emergency response; 100 mSv (10 rem) for protecting valuable property; 250 mSv (25 rem) for life-saving operations or preventing catastrophic conditions; with informed consent up to 500 mSv (50 rem) under clearly defined extraordinary circumstances. Acute radiation syndrome thresholds: 1 Sv (100 rem) prodromal nausea/vomiting; 2 Sv hematopoietic syndrome with lymphocyte depletion 12-48 h post-exposure; 4 Sv LD50 without medical intervention; 8 Sv near-universal mortality despite intensive care; 30 Sv neurological/cardiovascular syndrome with rapid death. Practical field rules: dosimeter on every responder; turn around at 50 mSv accumulated unless life-saving; rotate teams to minimize individual dose; prioritize Time / Distance / Shielding before any approach. Triage post-exposure: lymphocyte count at 24, 48, 72 h serves as biological dosimeter — drop to <500/uL by 24 h indicates >2 Sv exposure and need for hematopoietic supportive care including filgrastim/pegfilgrastim, platelet/RBC transfusion, antibiotics for febrile neutropenia.
